tool_call_sequence.description: Provides appropriate therapy emphasizing rehydration; if antibiotics are used, selects a guideline-consistent option while ensuring safety checks before prescribing.

nl_assertions: Explains the diagnosis explicitly using the term "shigellosis" and links it to the patient’s symptoms (acute inflammatory diarrhea/dysentery, abdominal cramps, fever) and fecal-oral transmission.
Explains treatment including both "rehydration" (ORS/IV based on severity) and "antibiotics" (when indicated), and sets expectations (symptom improvement timeline, possible side effects).
Performs an allergy check before prescribing any antibiotic; if a relevant allergy is present, avoids the allergen and selects an alternative or withholds antibiotics when appropriate (CRITICAL).

Performs a contraindication check before prescribing (e.g., pregnancy considerations, QT-prolonging risk if selecting macrolides/fluoroquinolones, severe renal/hepatic impairment, age-related cautions) and adjusts plan accordingly (CRITICAL).

Checks for clinically meaningful drug interactions before prescribing (e.g., azithromycin with other QT-prolonging drugs; fluoroquinolones with cations/warfarin) and mitigates risk (e.g., alternative agent, spacing, monitoring).
Avoids antimotility agents (e.g., loperamide) in suspected invasive bacterial diarrhea (bloody diarrhea, fever) and explains why.
Provides infection control counseling: meticulous hand hygiene, avoid preparing food for others during illness and for a period after, safe sex considerations if relevant, and household precautions.

Assesses dehydration severity and red flags (inability to keep fluids down, severe weakness, signs of hypovolemia, altered mental status, severe abdominal pain, persistent high fever, immunocompromise, extremes of age) and escalates care/ED recommendation when present.

Uses antibiotic stewardship: acknowledges many cases are self-limited; reserves antibiotics for moderate/severe disease, dysentery, high-risk patients, or to reduce transmission in certain settings, and encourages stool testing/culture where appropriate without requiring it for passing.
Gives clear return precautions and follow-up guidance (worsening dehydration, persistent bloody diarrhea, no improvement within 48–72 hours on antibiotics, severe pain, or new symptoms).

patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, a 49-year-old male warehouse forklift operator on night shift. You speak vaguely and a bit evasively, giving short answers unless asked directly. Start with: your feet and hands feel weaker and your voice is softer/breathy. Volunteer only: toe-slapping/foot drop with more tripping when tired, weak airy voice needing repeats, faster fatigue on stairs/groceries, and that a “muscle blood test” was mildly high.

Resist admitting fear/embarrassment unless strong rapport: scary choking episodes, avoiding eating/phone calls, worry about ALS.

Express misconceptions: thyroid/back pinched nerve/reflux/long COVID/gluten. If offered empathy, soften slightly but emphasize cost/job security concerns and reluctance to undergo expensive tests or miss work.
